AI-assisted healthcare works best when it supports clinicians — not replaces them. Human oversight, secure patient data, and responsible deployment remain at the center of modern medical care. (Photo: created with AI)

2 min read
Just now

--

A Salesforce report shows that patients are willing to use AI agents in healthcare when there is a responsible institution behind them and a clear option to speak with a person.
Salesforce published a new edition of its Connected Health Consumer Report, based on a survey of 3,200 adults across eight countries. Patients are three times more likely to trust an AI agent integrated into their doctor’s secure portal than a public chatbot.
Trust depends on who provides the system, what data it uses and who is accountable when something goes wrong. The report also shows that 90% of patients want human supervision for AI-based medical support. For administrative tasks, 89% want a clear option to escalate to a person.
Trust depends on context
Medical AI is often presented through accuracy. Better diagnosis. Better recommendation. Better assistance. For a patient, the same agent can feel different depending on where it appears.
The channel changes trust. An agent inside a doctor’s portal has an institution behind it. A public chatbot usually leaves more questions about data use and responsibility if it gives a wrong answer.
Patients accept help, but set limits
Salesforce says 61% of patients globally feel comfortable using agentic AI in healthcare contexts. It also says 64% would share their full medical history with AI to get a faster diagnosis.
That openness comes from friction in the system. According to the document shared by Salesforce, 58% of patients delay or avoid necessary care because scheduling an appointment is too complicated. Sixty-six percent have run out of medication while waiting for a prescription renewal approval.
The critical point is accountability
The main concerns mentioned in the report are accuracy and data privacy. Thirty-six percent cite the accuracy of diagnosis or treatment as their top concern. Thirty percent mention the privacy and security of their health information.
Sources consulted
Salesforce: “Connected Health Consumer Report 2026”
Press document received: “New research. Patients trust their doctors’ AI agents three times more than public AI”
